name: Nat Adderley
description: American jazz cornet and trumpet player (1931–2000)
abstract: Nathaniel Carlyle Adderley (November 25, 1931 – January 2, 2000) was an American jazz trumpeter and composer. He was the younger brother of saxophonist Julian "Cannonball" Adderley, with whom he played for many years.

about_quotes.speaker: Keter Betts
about_quotes.evidence: There was Jimmy Campbell on drums, Vince Guaraldi on piano, Bill Harris on trombone, and Nat Adderley and Charlie Byrd and myself.
about_quotes.doc_title: Keter Betts interviewed by Monk Rowe, Sarasota, Florida, April 11, 1996

about_quotes.speaker: Chuck Findley
about_quotes.evidence: And I listened to Lee Morgan, I listened to Kenny Dorham, I listened to — after Bobby Hackett this is. But Bobby Hackett was the one that got me playing like lyrical and tunes and stuff like that. And then I listened to Lee Morgan and like I said, and Nat Adderley I listened to as well.
about_quotes.doc_title: Chuck Findley interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, September 6, 2023

about_quotes.speaker: Longineu Parsons
about_quotes.evidence: Nat raised me as a musician... Nat said, 'Look you stick close to me' you know, 'I’ll look out for you.' And he called my mother and told her the same thing and said, 'Look, you don’t let him sign any contract without talking to me.'
about_quotes.doc_title: Longineu Parsons interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, February 17, 2026

about_quotes.speaker: Orrin Keepnews
about_quotes.evidence: Cannon put this thing together with his dates for the trumpet section was Sweets Edison, Clark Terry, Nat Adderley... They’re produced by Cannon, Nat and me. And actually we did work together on it.
about_quotes.doc_title: Orrin Keepnews interviewed by Monk Rowe, San Francisco, California, August 7, 2002

about_quotes.speaker: JJ Johnson
about_quotes.evidence: Nat and I became neighbors... We really became fast friends then and there... traveling around together and performing together and recording together.
about_quotes.doc_title: JJ Johnson — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Hubert Laws
about_quotes.evidence: Mongo did one of his last records up there at Riverside, along with Nat Adderley playing trumpet on a tune that I wrote called Bembe Blue.
about_quotes.doc_title: Hubert Laws — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
